summary: Matched the ShieldBreakFall-related portion of ft_08A4 by moving several functions out of asm into C and adding dedicated ShieldBreakFall/ShieldBreakDown source and header files. The PR report shows src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.c at 100.00% for ftCo_80098D90, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Anim, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_IASA, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Phys, and ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Coll. One remaining transition helper, ftCo_80098E3C, was placed in ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.c as MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly, preserving exact codegen while still relocating it from the monolithic asm file.
what_changed: Removed the assembly definitions for ftCo_80098D90, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Anim, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_IASA, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Phys, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Coll, and ftCo_80098E3C from asm/melee/ft/ft_08A4.s.
Added src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.c with matched C implementations for the ShieldBreakFall entry, animation, IASA, physics, and collision callbacks.
Added src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.h with prototypes for ftCo_80098D90 and the ShieldBreakFall callback functions.
Added src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.c containing ftCo_80098E3C as MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly plus float constants ftCo_804D8660 = 0 and ftCo_804D8664 = 1.
Added src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.h and moved the ftCo_80098E3C prototype out of src/melee/ft/ft_08A4.h into the more specific ftCommon shield-break header.
Updated ftCo_ShieldBreakFly.c to include ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.h and ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.h.
Updated obj_files.mk to compile ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.c.o and ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.c.o before the remaining ft_08A4.s.o.
The PR title names ftCo_ShieldBreakFly, but the diff and report evidence primarily cover ShieldBreakFall and ShieldBreakDown support code.
smart_moves: Split matched code into focused ftCommon action files instead of leaving the functions in the broad ft_08A4 assembly/header namespace.
Used small, idiomatic C wrappers for simple callbacks: empty Anim/IASA functions, Phys forwarding to ft_80084EEC, and Coll forwarding to ft_80082C74 with ftCo_80098E3C as the callback.

Expressed the motion-state transition in ftCo_80098D90 with named constants such as ftCo_MS_ShieldBreakFall and Ft_MF_* flags instead of raw immediate values, improving readability while matching the original code.
Kept ftCo_80098E3C as inline assembly under MWERKS_GEKKO, which allowed the object to move into C source organization without requiring a full C match for a harder helper function.
Introduced local named sdata2 float constants for the inline-asm helper, replacing anonymous .L_804D8660/.L_804D8664 labels from the asm file.
decomp_lessons: When a cluster contains both easy wrappers and a stubborn helper, it can still be useful to decompile the easy functions to C and isolate the remaining hard function as MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly in the correct source file.
Simple fighter action callbacks often match cleanly as empty functions or direct forwarding wrappers; preserving exact callback signatures matters for table integration and collision helper calls.

Motion-state calls should prefer known enum and flag names where available; ftCo_80098D90 shows raw state 0xCE and flag 0x10D4 represented as ftCo_MS_ShieldBreakFall and a composed Ft_MF_* flag expression.
Header cleanup should accompany asm-to-C movement: broad catch-all headers like ft_08A4.h should shed prototypes once a function has a more specific module header.
Build ordering in obj_files.mk matters when peeling functions out of an asm file; new C object files must be inserted before the remaining asm object so symbols resolve in the intended object layout.
PR titles may lag the actual slice contents; for future lookup, this PR should be searched under ShieldBreakFall and ftCo_80098D90 as well as ShieldBreakFly.
naming_conventions: New ftCommon files follow the action-specific pattern ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.c/.h and ftCo_ShieldBreakDown.c/.h.
Known callbacks use descriptive action suffixes: ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Anim, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_IASA, ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Phys, and ftCo_ShieldBreakFall_Coll.
Unknown or not-yet-named helpers retain address-based names such as ftCo_80098D90 and ftCo_80098E3C.
Header include guards in the new files use GALE01 address-style names, specifically GALE01_0988E4 and GALE01_098E3C.
Small data constants moved from anonymous asm labels to ftCo-prefixed exported constants such as ftCo_804D8660 and ftCo_804D8664.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Removed 104 lines of corresponding PowerPC assembly from ft_08A4.s after introducing C and inline-asm replacements.

Matched ftCo_80098D90 as C by loading fp from gobj->user_data, calling Fighter_ChangeMotionState with NULL, 0, 1, 0 float/argument values, then calling ftCommon_8007D468(fp) and ftCommon_8007EBAC(fp, 8, 0).
Matched no-op callbacks as empty C functions for Anim and IASA.
Matched physics and collision callbacks as direct calls to ft_80084EEC and ft_80082C74 respectively.
Used MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly with nofralloc, original instruction comments, and local lbl_ labels for ftCo_80098E3C where C was not provided in the diff.

Preserved the original ftCo_80098E3C control flow in inline asm: check fighter field 0xE0 against 1, optionally call ftCommon_8007D7FC, call ftCo_80097570 to choose motion state 0xCF or 0xD0, call Fighter_ChangeMotionState with flag 0x1094, then call ftCo_800978D4.
review_feedback: No review comments or reviewer feedback were present in the provided PR context.
The automated report excerpt shows 100.00% file matching for src/melee/ft/chara/ftCommon/ftCo_ShieldBreakFall.c, with zero score for all listed functions.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that replace the MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly implementation of ftCo_80098E3C with matched C.
Search for later renames of ftCo_80098D90 or ftCo_80098E3C to descriptive ShieldBreakFall/ShieldBreakDown names.
Look for nearby shield-break action PRs that complete the remaining functions in asm/melee/ft/ft_08A4.s.
Check whether the PR title's ShieldBreakFly wording corresponds to dependencies in ftCo_ShieldBreakFly.c or was simply broader than the matched functions.
